entities.name: TRADE-OFFS
entities.description: The balance between conflicting goals or outcomes in a system

entities.name: DO NO HARM
entities.description: A principle ensuring that interventions do not cause unintended negative impacts

entities.name: SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS
entities.description: Global goals established by the UN for sustainable development

entities.name: IMPACT PROCESSES
entities.description: The mechanisms and pathways through which interventions lead to specific outcomes

entities.name: AGRI-FOOD SYSTEMS
entities.description: The complex network of food production, processing, distribution, and consumption

entities.name: AGRICULTURAL PROGRAMMING
entities.description: The planning and implementation of agricultural development initiatives

entities.name: SINGLE POINT INTERVENTIONS
entities.description: Interventions that target a specific, isolated point in a system rather than the whole system

entities.name: DEVELOPMENT PARADIGMS
entities.description: Theoretical frameworks and beliefs that guide development interventions

entities.name: SYSTEMIC CHANGE
entities.description: Fundamental transformation of the structures and functions of a system

entities.name: PRO-POOR VALUE CHAINS
entities.description: Value chains specifically designed to increase the income and well-being of poor producers

entities.name: NUTRITION-SENSITIVE VALUE CHAINS
entities.description: Value chains designed to improve the availability and accessibility of nutritious foods

entities.name: GREENING OF VALUE CHAINS
entities.description: The process of making value chains more environmentally sustainable

entities.name: INTERVENTION DESIGN
entities.description: The process of planning and structuring a development intervention

entities.name: EVALUATION
entities.description: The systematic assessment of the design, implementation, and results of an intervention

relationships.description: Managing trade-offs is necessary to achieve do no harm outcomes
The do no harm principle is central to achieving the SDGs
Achieving SDGs requires an understanding of impact processes
Impact processes occur within the context of agri-food systems
Agricultural programming often relies on single point interventions
Single point interventions are framed by earlier development paradigms
Earlier development paradigms are often at odds with systemic change goals
Systemic change is a primary goal of the SDGs
An agri-food systems lens is used to highlight trade-offs in various value chain interventions
Trade-offs exist within interventions for pro-poor value chains
Trade-offs exist within interventions for nutrition-sensitive value chains
Trade-offs exist within interventions for the greening of value chains
The do no harm principle should be embedded in intervention design
The do no harm principle should be embedded in evaluation
